headline: Molly Graham on giving away your Legos: the next pile is always behind the cliff
digest_md:
Molly Graham — early Google, Facebook (Chan Zuckerberg Initiative under Zuck), scaling Quip with Bret Taylor, now running Glue Club for operators in fast-changing companies — has built her practice around one paradox: *to grow with your company, you have to keep handing back what you've gotten good at.*

The core frameworks:

- **Give away your Legos.** *'You have to grow as fast as your company is growing if you really want to take advantage, both learning to give away what you've gotten good at and move on to the next shiny pile of Legos.'* The reason this hurts: you got good at the old pile and it feels like yours. The reason you do it anyway: the next pile is always more interesting and only available to people who've already let go.
- **The J-curve vs. the stairs.** Chamath drew her two career paths on a whiteboard: the stairs (predictable promotions every two years, boring), or the J-curve — jump off the cliff, fall for 6-9 months, then climb out far higher than the stairs ever reached. The J-curve is what you choose when you give away your Legos.
- **Bob the monster.** Every change — getting layered, a new hire, losing a project — comes with a wave of emotion that says *send the rage email, push them over, take it back*. Molly externalized that voice into a character named Bob. *'Bob's job is to make me the worst version of myself.'* You don't need to make Bob go away; you need to not act on him.
- **The two-week rule.** *'These emotions do not go away in 24 hours.'* Most reactions roll through in days. Anything still there after two weeks is real signal — go talk to someone about it.
- **Embrace change, step into the future.** *'Holding onto things almost always leads us to the worst version of ourselves.'* You can't see around the corner; the move is to step into it.

For the mid-level PM: this is a week-by-week practice. Which Lego are you holding because it's familiar? What email is Bob trying to send? What change are you fighting that you should be stepping into?
takeaways: Look at your weekly calendar this week and find one Lego you've held too long because you're good at it. Give it to a teammate by Friday — not because you must, but because the next pile only opens once you let go.

If you're about to send a strongly-worded email about a reorg, a layering, or a new hire — name the voice telling you to send it (Bob, or your name for it). Wait 14 days. If it's still right at day 14, send it then.
When you get layered or someone new takes a chunk of your scope, your first job is not to complain — it's to look forward and ask what you'd rather be doing in 12 months that this opens up.
If you've been on the same stairs for 3+ years, the J-curve question is fair: would a deliberate fall for 6-9 months take you somewhere the stairs can't?

source.title: The high-growth handbook: Molly Graham’s frameworks for leading through chaos, change, and scale

questions.scenario_md:
You're the PM who built and still owns the team's onboarding flow — your most-loved feature, the one your manager complimented in your last review. A new APM joined three months ago and is competent. You're spending ~30% of your week on onboarding edits a younger PM could handle. The team's roadmap has two new surfaces no one is leading.

Per Molly Graham's framing, the most accurate read on your week is —

options.text: Healthy ownership — keep onboarding while you mentor the APM through the next quarter.
You're holding a Lego you've gotten good at; the next pile is one of the unowned surfaces, and the APM is the one who should pick up onboarding.
Time-management issue — block your calendar so onboarding work fits in 10% of the week.
Org gap — escalate that the team needs another PM hire before redistributing scope.

questions.explanation_md: Graham's central frame: to grow with the company, you must hand back what you've gotten good at. Holding the comfortable Lego is the failure mode, not the prudent move. Time-management and headcount fixes preserve the hold; the right move is to give it away.
questions.pm_takeaway: By Friday, hand off your most-comfortable Lego to the APM and pick one of the unowned surfaces. Tell your manager you've done it; don't ask permission.

citation.title: The high-growth handbook: Molly Graham’s frameworks for leading through chaos, change, and scale
citation.quote_excerpt: You have to grow as fast as your company is growing if you really want to take advantage, both learning to give away what you’ve gotten good at and move on to the next shiny pile of Legos.

questions.scenario_md:
Friday 9:00 PM. You just learned your peer was promoted to Group PM and you weren't. Your draft Slack to your manager: 'I think we need to talk about how decisions get made on this team.' You're about to hit send.

Per Molly Graham's framing, the missing step before the send button is —

options.text: Sleep on it; you'll feel better tomorrow morning.
Sharpen the message; passive-aggressive Slacks land worse than direct ones.
Recognize this is your inner monster (Graham calls hers Bob); let the wave roll through, don't act, and revisit in two weeks.
Send it Monday morning instead so it lands during work hours.

questions.explanation_md: Graham's diagnostic: emotions like the urge to send the rage email at 9:00 PM are normal but not useful — they don't go away by morning, and Bob's job is to make you the worst version of yourself. The two-week rule says wait; if it's still real at day 14, then it's signal, not Bob.
questions.pm_takeaway: Save the draft. Give it 14 days. If you still want to send it on day 14 with a clear head, then send a sharper version — direct, not passive-aggressive.

citation.title: The high-growth handbook: Molly Graham’s frameworks for leading through chaos, change, and scale
citation.quote_excerpt: Bob’s job is... Bob’s the one that wants to send the rage emails at 9:00PM and burn the house down. And the thing to learn about Bob is that, like I said, Bob never goes away. Bob is someone that you have to learn to deal with.

questions.scenario_md:
You just learned you're being layered: a new senior PM is being hired above you to manage your area. Your first instinct is to pull together a memo to your skip-level explaining why this is a mistake.

Applying Graham's 'embrace change, step into the future' framing, the action you take this week is —

options.text: Send the memo Monday — make the case while the decision is still fresh.
Quietly start interviewing externally; the layering signals you've plateaued here.
Stay heads-down on your current scope; the new PM will have to prove themselves before anything changes.
Schedule 30 minutes with the incoming senior PM to ask what they want to learn from you and what they want you to own next.

questions.explanation_md: Graham's frame is that holding onto the past leads us to the worst version of ourselves and that being layered often ends up being great even when you can't see it. Stepping into the future means using the new manager as new surface area, not fighting their arrival.
questions.pm_takeaway: Within 7 days of any reorg news, book a 30-minute meeting with whoever is now adjacent to your scope. Ask what they're trying to learn — that's your new pile of Legos.

citation.title: The high-growth handbook: Molly Graham’s frameworks for leading through chaos, change, and scale
citation.quote_excerpt: step into the future and let the past go and see what you’re going to learn. And sometimes you’ll learn that it’s time to leave or that this isn’t the right pile of Legos for you.

questions.scenario_md:
You've been a senior PM at a profitable Series-D for three years. You got promoted on schedule, your reviews are strong, you know the playbook cold. Two opportunities arrive the same week:

1. Lateral move to GPM at the same company, same area, predictable workload.
2. Founding PM #1 at an early-stage AI startup, total chaos, lower title.
3. Stay; your manager hints another promotion is coming in 18 months.
4. Sabbatical to think.

Per Graham's J-curve framing, the option that fits is —

options.text: Option 3 — predictable promotion in 18 months is the highest-EV path.
Option 1 — same company, broader scope, manageable risk.
Option 2 — jump off the cliff knowing you'll fall for 6-9 months and likely climb out higher than the stairs reach.
Option 4 — clarity first, then commit.

questions.explanation_md: Graham's J-curve is the deliberate fall: you take the role you can't immediately do, fall for 6-9 months, and climb out beyond what the stairs would have reached. After three predictable years, the curve is the choice that grows you faster than your company will.
questions.pm_takeaway: If you've been on the stairs 3+ years, write down what your six-month J-curve fall would look like and what you'd learn climbing out. Bring it to your next 1:1.

citation.title: The high-growth handbook: Molly Graham’s frameworks for leading through chaos, change, and scale
citation.quote_excerpt: The much more fun careers are like jumping off cliffs. Basically, that you jump off this thing and you do fall for a period of time. I always like to say it’s about six to nine months, but then this thing happens where you climb out.

questions.scenario_md:
Three weeks ago a new VP joined and reorganized your area. Initially you were upset; that has not faded. You catch yourself bringing it up to peers, dreading planning meetings, and resenting the new VP's reviews of your PRDs. Tomorrow you have your first formal 1:1 with them.

The response that best translates Graham's two-week rule for your 1:1 is —

options.text: Stay friendly and superficial; venting in a first 1:1 will damage the relationship.
Bring it up directly: 'Three weeks in, this still bothers me. Here's specifically what, and here's what I'd want different.' Past 14 days = real signal.
Soften it: 'Some folks on the team have concerns about the reorg' — surface without owning.
Skip the 1:1 entirely and request a transfer first.

questions.explanation_md: Graham's two-week rule: emotions that last past 14 days are real, not Bob. The translation for the 1:1 is to take ownership of the feeling, name it specifically, and propose what you'd want different — not to deflect, soften, or escape.
questions.pm_takeaway: If a workplace feeling has lasted more than two weeks, say it directly in your next 1:1 — name what, name what you'd want different, own it. That's the test for Bob vs. signal.

citation.title: The high-growth handbook: Molly Graham’s frameworks for leading through chaos, change, and scale
citation.quote_excerpt: anything that lasts longer than two weeks is actually something you should pay attention to. It’s something that if it’s been around for two weeks, it’s something you should go talk to someone about.
